content:
The minimal CSS that gets a dashboard, a docs page, or a blog looking serious — box model, flex/grid, color, type, and what to ignore.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You’re a backend developer. Sooner or later you have to put a face on something — a docs site, an internal dashboard, a personal blog — and that means CSS, a subject deep enough to be someone’s entire career. You don’t have a career’s worth of time for it.
The good news, well put in matklad’s writeup on the subject, is that modern CSS has a small, learnable subset that’s genuinely enough for a simple page. The bad news is a handful of gotchas that will quietly wreck your layout in ways you’d never suspect, and cost you an afternoon of debugging. This is the subset worth learning and the traps worth knowing — enough to style a page without falling down the rabbit hole.
Start with semantic HTML and classless CSS
The first instinct of someone copying “real” websites is to wrap everything in nested <div>s with classes. Don’t. For a simple page, lean on HTML’s semantic elements — main, header, nav, article, footer, code, kbd — and then write CSS that styles those elements directly. No classes required.
